Boxing News

How to Buy Boxing Tickets: Avoiding Scams and Finding Real Value

ByDexter Okafor|8 February 2025  ·  5 min read

Major boxing events at the O2 and AO Arena sell out within minutes of going on sale. (Getty Images)

Boxing ticket scams are on the rise. Here's our guide to the safest platforms, how to spot a fake ticket, and where to find the best prices.

Why Boxing Is the Sport Most Targeted by Ticket Fraudsters

According to Action Fraud UK, boxing events accounted for 31% of all sports ticket fraud reported in the last 12 months — more than any other single sport. The primary reason is the concentration of large, one-night-only events with massive public demand and a relatively short promotional window. Fights like Anthony Joshua's rematches and Tyson Fury's heavyweight bouts generate millions of social media impressions within minutes of announcement, and fraudsters exploit the urgency by listing fake tickets before official sales even open.

How to Spot a Fraudulent Boxing Ticket Listing

Several warning signs are consistent across fraudulent listings. Price anomalies are the most common indicator: if a ringside seat is listed at 40% below every other platform, the ticket is almost certainly counterfeit or the seller is phishing for payment details. Be extremely cautious of sellers asking for payment via bank transfer, PayPal Friends and Family, or cryptocurrency — none offer purchase protection. Screenshots of PDF tickets sent via WhatsApp are easily duplicated and can be sold hundreds of times. Always insist on QR-code mobile transfer through a named platform.

The Safest Platforms for Buying Boxing Tickets in the UK

For major UK promotions (Matchroom, Queensberry), the safest starting point is always the official promoter's ticketing partner — typically AXS or FootballTicketNet. Both operate verified transfer systems that link tickets to the buyer's account and are unduplicatable. For the secondary market, FootballTicketNet and Awin both offer money-back guarantees if tickets are invalid at entry.

Getting the Best Price: Timing and Strategy

Boxing ticket prices follow a distinctive pattern driven by fight announcements and media cycles. The first 24 hours after an announcement are always the most expensive as demand spikes before supply adjusts. Prices typically soften 10–14 days post-announcement as initial urgency fades. For undercard and non-title fights, checking official channels on the afternoon before the event often reveals last-minute face-value releases. For premium ringside seats at major shows, prices historically bottom out around one week before fight night as unsold premium inventory gets marked down aggressively.
